children.text: Art and cultural expression are central to how Afro-Peruvian women articulate identity, memory, and resistance. Beyond formal politics, music, dance, and performance become spaces where Blackness is affirmed and reimagined. These practices challenge dominant representations that have historically marginalized or stereotyped Afro-Peruvian bodies and cultures.

, Victoria Santa Cruz reflects on the relationship between rhythm, the body, and Black identity. Through her dialogue with Eugenio Barba, she emphasizes how the body carries memory and history, transforming performance into a form of knowledge. For Santa Cruz,
rhythm is not only artistic expression but a way of reconnecting with ancestral
identity and reclaiming dignity.

Santa Cruz positions rhythm as her call to action, allowing her to reaffirm her Black and female identity beyond colonial narratives that render Blackness nonexistent in the Peruvian context. Rhythm operates as an Afro-Peruvian framework for liberation, alternative realities, and knowledge production beyond the politics of miscegenation and whitening. Rather than functioning as a musical structure, rhythm operates as a dynamic unity that generates a process of becoming that exceeds Western colonial notions of individualism and racial hierarchies. Later, in 2004, Santa Cruz published her only book "Rythm the Eternal Organizer" where she expanded her theory about rhythm

This perspective resonates with Black feminist approaches that understand the body as a site of knowledge and resistance. Through art, Afro-Peruvian women do not simply represent their experiences they actively reshape how Blackness is understood and lived. Artistic practice becomes a
space where memory, identity, and resistance converge.

title: "Ser afroperuana" is resisting through art and rhythm
